purpose: Behavior change frameworks and coaching models for the personal-coach skill. Used to design evidence-based weekly experiments and tracking plans.

tiny_habits.author: BJ Fogg, Stanford Behavior Design Lab
tiny_habits.core_principle: Make the behavior tiny enough that motivation is irrelevant. Anchor it to an existing habit. Celebrate immediately after.
tiny_habits.formula: After I [ANCHOR HABIT], I will [TINY BEHAVIOR].
examples: After I pour my morning coffee, I will write one sentence about my top priority.
After I sit down at my desk, I will review my three most important tasks.
tiny_habits.use_when: User has tried and failed to build a habit — reduce friction first

implementation_intentions.author: Peter Gollwitzer, NYU
implementation_intentions.core_principle: Specifying when, where, and how to act dramatically increases follow-through (2–3x). The brain pre-programs the response.
examples: When it is 9:00 AM on weekdays, I will spend 15 minutes on my most important task before checking email.
When I finish a meeting, I will spend 2 minutes writing the key decision in my notes.
implementation_intentions.use_when: User has a clear intention but fails to act consistently — add specificity

identity_based_habits.author: James Clear, Atomic Habits
identity_based_habits.core_principle: Behavior change is identity change. Vote for the identity you want with small consistent actions. 'I am a person who...'
identity_based_habits.formula: Every time I [BEHAVIOR], I cast a vote for being a person who [IDENTITY].
examples: Every time I prepare before a meeting, I cast a vote for being a person who leads with clarity.
Every time I give direct feedback, I cast a vote for being a trustworthy manager.
identity_based_habits.use_when: User is trying to shift their professional identity (e.g., individual contributor → leader)

if_then_planning.core_principle: Pre-decide responses to obstacles. Reduces decision fatigue and prevents good intentions from collapsing under pressure.
examples: If I feel like skipping my planning session, then I will do a 5-minute version instead.
If a meeting goes long and I miss my writing window, then I will take 10 minutes before lunch.
if_then_planning.use_when: User has a specific known obstacle (busy schedule, energy dips, interruptions)

signals: Activities the user describes with energy or enthusiasm
Tasks they complete faster than peers seem to
Areas where they are sought out for advice
Work they find easy that others find difficult
blind_spot_signals: Strengths used too intensely become liabilities (e.g., analytical rigor → analysis paralysis)
Skills the user undervalues because they feel easy

leverage_point_analysis.description: Identify the one behavior or habit that, if changed, would improve multiple other outcomes
high_leverage_signals: A pattern that appears in multiple different problem descriptions
A constraint that blocks multiple goals simultaneously
A habit that, if changed, would build momentum across areas

bottleneck_thinking.description: Adapted from Goldratt's Theory of Constraints for personal productivity
bottleneck_thinking.question: What is the one constraint that, if removed, would make everything else easier or faster?
bottleneck_thinking.use_when: User describes being overwhelmed or stuck across multiple areas

daily_binary.description: Simplest tracker — did you do it or not?

daily_scaled.description: Rate quality or effort, not just completion
daily_scaled.use_when: User wants to understand patterns (energy, context) not just compliance

outcome_tracking.description: Track the leading indicator AND a lagging outcome

example.weekly_outcome_signal: Number of projects moved forward meaningfully this week

coaching_principles.one_change_rule: Always propose exactly one behavior change. Multiple changes dilute focus and reduce adherence.
coaching_principles.low_friction_rule: The change should take <15 minutes per day. Ambitious changes fail. Small consistent ones compound.
coaching_principles.testable_rule: The change must be testable — observable, countable, or binary (did/did not). Vague intentions are not changes.
coaching_principles.strengths_first: Lead with what the user does well. Identify the opportunity hidden in the strength before addressing the gap.
coaching_principles.non_judgmental: Describe patterns, not character. 'You tend to delay difficult decisions' not 'You are avoidant.'

typical_experiments: Prepare 1 crisp point before each meeting
Send a one-line follow-up after key conversations
Lead with the ask before the context in emails
Identify top 3 priorities each morning before opening email
Block 1 hour of uninterrupted work daily
Use a NOT-to-do list for low-value recurring tasks
Send a weekly progress note to manager on Fridays
Speak first in the next 3 team meetings
Present one insight or result at each team sync
Set a 24-hour decision timer for non-reversible choices
Use a 2-question filter: reversible? high stakes?
Write the decision and rationale in one sentence before acting

signals: Doing work that could be delegated
typical_experiments: Identify one recurring task to delegate this week
Coach instead of correct on the next team error
Define a clear outcome and hand off the 'how'
